text: This is Logan Meller with National Surgery Review. Thank you for joining us today as we walk you through a laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication. Preceding this procedure, we typically start with a laparoscopic hiatal hernia repair.

The surgeon begins the dissection by mobilizing the greater curvature of the stomach, using an advanced energy device to take down the short gastric vessels. A lateral assistant port is utilized to enhance exposure and facilitate the dissection process.

The surgeon approaches the spleen, sharply dissecting posterior adhesions to the stomach to enhance its mobility and improve visualization near the splenic hilum. The short gastric vessels between the stomach and spleen are carefully taken down without using thermal energy.

With the stomach fully mobilized to the level of the left crus and the hiatal hernia repaired, the surgeon begins a loose fundoplication. A marking stitch is placed at the fundoplication site to prevent a body wrap. Under direct visualization, a 50 to 56 French bougie is inserted. The surgeon uses a grasper to pull the marking stitch behind the esophagus, employing the shoe shine technique to work the stomach back and forth, ensuring a loose, floppy fundoplication.

The surgeon secures the fundoplication using three interrupted non-absorbable sutures, tying them extracorporeally for enhanced knot security. The marking stitch is removed after placing the initial suture.

The assistant port holds down the stomach while the surgeon secures the fundoplication with three stitches, creating a wrap approximately three centimeters in length. After removing the bougie, an intraoperative EGD is performed to confirm the anatomy. A probe is passed under the wrap to ensure the fundoplication is loose and floppy.
